Saturday Summary: Fireball leads Snitzel’s Randwick stakes sweep

Trainer Dan Morton collected his third G2 Karrakatta Plate in four years with exciting juvenile Afireofgidgeecoals, and the son of Too Darn Hot burned the unbeaten record of the next two home. Home Affairs added a new stakes winner in New Zealand with Miss Janet, and two exciting prospects were unearthed with The Speed Machine and Silken Salute.

Donato Lanni, bidding on behalf of Saudi businessman Amr Zedan, went to all-time high US$10.5 million to acquire a colt by champion Flightline from the Hartley/DeRenzo Thoroughbred consignment. The colt lifted the Ocala Spring Breeze Up sale to records in all metrics.

The last three winners of the G1 All Aged Stakes face off to find out if any of them can be a dual winner on Saturday, while Adrian Bott thinks top maiden Miss Chanel can break her duck in the G1 Champagne Stakes. Meanwhile, brother Private Eye and King's Secret battle it out in the G3 Hall Mark Stakes.

The last yearling sale in the Inglis calendar, the Inglis HTBA Yearling Sale, takes place at the Riverside Stables Sales Complex on Sunday. The Inglis team are cautious on how the value end of the market will perform in the current economic environment, whilst remaining confident there is plenty to interest buyers in the sale.

The Federation of Bloodstock Agents Australia have announced a trio of mentees for their 2026 Mentorship Program, which aims to give people with a true passion for bloodstock a formal path into joining the industry.

With vibrant trade throughout the day, the Ocala Breeders' Sales Company's Spring 2-Year-Olds in Training Sale concluded its third session well ahead of its 2025 renewal and on track for a record-setting gross, average and median ahead of the auction's final session. A filly by Bolt d'Oro became the auction's fourth million-dollar juvenile when purchased by Bill Childs's CSLR for $1.3 million.

The numbers from this season's juvenile stakes results say something the industry already suspects but rarely states plainly: the yearling sales ring is, by some distance, the single biggest predictor of whether a horse wins a stakes race at two.

Five winners by his direct descendants. Eight more carrying his blood through the dam. Danehill hasn't been near a breeding shed in over two decades - and he still dominated the biggest carnival of the Australian racing year.
